summary: McKenzie debunks myths from both the open source and micro-ISV communities about their supposed incompatibility. He argues that open source and small commercial software vendors coexist naturally because they serve fundamentally different needs and audiences. Most OSS is built by corporate-funded developers for programmer needs, while uISVs serve non-technical niches that OSS ignores. The real competitive moat for uISVs isn't code complexity but marketing, documentation, and user-facing work that OSS developers avoid. He uses his own Bingo Card Creator as a case study, showing how its open source competitor languishes despite being technically viable, because nobody is motivated to do the unglamorous work of reaching actual users.
key_points: Most major open source projects are primarily funded and developed by large corporations, not volunteer hobbyists working out of altruism
Programmers are great at building software for their own needs but poor at building for non-technical users, creating permanent niches for commercial software
The overwhelming majority of open source projects are inactive and abandoned, making the 'many eyes' benefit largely theoretical
Writing the software is roughly 10% of the battle — marketing, documentation, and user support are where uISVs create durable competitive advantage
OSS developers won't clone niche commercial software because the work is boring and the domains are too obscure to motivate intrinsic contribution
money_quotes: Programmers are very good at producing software for their own needs… and a lot less good at producing software for other's needs.

Most successful uISVs have long since learned the lesson that writing the program is about 10% of the battle: you've then got to get it in front of prospective users, write documentation, help them with their problems, market market and market some more.
Intrinsic motivation only takes the typical OSS developer as far as closing the IDE.
Here's a dirty little secret: most big-name OSS projects are produced primarily by programmers at large corporations working at the direction of their bosses in exchange for cash money.
I'm sure any competent C developer could take the bingo-cards codebase and make it the equal of Bingo Card Creator in less than a man-week. I'm equally sure that they won't.
IBM is no more interested in bankrolling bingo-cards than they are interested in rolling out their own Proprietary Bingo Solution because the niche is just too tiny.
I have the strong suspicion that, as the Texas saying has it, they have a lot of hat but no cattle.
key_insight: Open source and commercial software don't compete because OSS developers lack the motivation to do the unglamorous marketing, documentation, and user-support work that makes niche software actually reach the people who need it.
